era_context: Early 14th century: The conflict between papal authority and secular rulers intensifies; the Yuan Dynasty in China faces succession instability; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; Mali Empire is rising in West Africa.

events.title: Conflict between Pope Boniface VIII and Philip IV of France escalates
events.description: The conflict between Pope Boniface VIII and Philip IV of France intensified in 1301 over issues of ecclesiastical taxation and papal authority. Philip IV sought to tax the French clergy without papal consent, while Boniface VIII asserted papal supremacy over secular rulers. The conflict would escalate dramatically in 1302–1303, culminating in the Anagni Incident. This dispute represented a fundamental challenge to medieval papal authority and the beginning of the papacy's decline as a major political force.
events.certainty_note: The escalation of the Boniface-Philip conflict in 1301 is well-documented in papal and French royal records.

events.title: Edward I of England campaigns in Scotland
events.description: Edward I of England, known as 'Hammer of the Scots,' continued his military campaigns to assert English control over Scotland. In 1301, Edward I launched a major campaign in Scotland as part of the ongoing conflict over Scottish independence. These campaigns would continue until Edward I's death in 1307, and the conflict would persist for decades, eventually leading to Scottish independence under Robert the Bruce.
events.certainty_note: Edward I's Scottish campaigns are well-documented in English royal records.

events.title: Mamluk-Ilkhanate border conflicts in Syria
events.description: Ongoing military conflicts between the Mamluk Sultanate and the Ilkhanate (Mongol state in Persia) characterized the early 1300s. Border skirmishes and raids occurred regularly as both powers competed for control of Syria and Mesopotamia. These conflicts would continue until the Ilkhanate's collapse in the 1330s.
events.certainty_note: Mamluk-Ilkhanate conflicts are well-documented but specific events in 1301 are not precisely dated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty succession disputes and internal instability
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty continued to face internal instability as various Mongol factions competed for control. By 1301, the succession was contested, with multiple claimants to the throne. This period of instability would continue until Temür Khan's consolidation in 1307. The Yuan Dynasty's internal conflicts weakened its ability to maintain control over China.
events.certainty_note: Yuan succession disputes are documented but specific events in 1301 are not precisely dated.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia under Osman I
events.description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman Empire, continued to expand Ottoman control over northwestern Anatolia through military campaigns against Byzantine territories. By 1301, the Ottomans had established themselves as a significant regional power and were beginning the process of conquest that would eventually lead to the fall of Constantinople.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman early history is poorly documented; specific events in 1301 are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. The Boniface-Philip conflict is well-documented in contemporary sources. Edward I's Scottish campaigns are confirmed in royal records. Mamluk, Yuan, and Ottoman developments are attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources, though specific dating for some events is approximate.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1301 is moderate but concentrated in European sources. The Boniface-Philip conflict and Edward I's Scottish campaigns are well-documented in English and papal records. Islamic sources (Mamluk and Ilkhanate) are substantial but often compiled later. Chinese sources exist but are filtered through Yuan imperial historiography. Ottoman sources are sparse and largely dependent on later compilations. West African sources are negligible. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe; developments in Asia and Africa are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The escalation of the Boniface-Philip conflict in 1301 led to the convening of the Estates-General in 1302.
Edward I's Scottish campaigns continued until his death in 1307.
